refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral by avoiding prescriptive terms like 'support' (which implies mechanism) and instead focusing on the outcome: maintaining communication integrity. The phrase 'customer-defined packet loss limits' and 'agreed network conditions' are preserved as acceptance criteria anchors without embedding implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, bandwidth constraints) and measure end-to-end packet loss across the communication path. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet loss statistics and any correlation with operator commands, video feed quality, or equipment response latency.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator commands from tele-operator to remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve intended control intent.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback from remote equipment to tele-operator with fidelity sufficient to enable safe situational awareness.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency anomalies) to the tele-operator and safety manager in real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and customer-configurable thresholds
DP2: Employ forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured network conditions
DP3: Establish dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and load balancing to reduce single-path loss impact
DP4: Deploy jitter buffering and packet reordering logic with timeout-based loss detection and on-demand retransmission
DP5: Implement network condition monitoring and dynamic codec/compression adjustment to reduce payload size and loss probability
DP6: Use a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission (for critical control frames) and FEC (for telemetry streams) with customer-tunable parameters
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a hybrid loss mitigation strategy that combines selective retransmission for critical control frames (operator commands, safety signals) with configurable forward error correction for telemetry and status streams, supported by continuous network condition monitoring and customer-defined loss thresholds that trigger adaptive parameter adjustment.

potential_sources: Coupling between loss detection logic and retransmission scheduling—mitigated by separating detection (passive monitoring) from action (configurable policy layer)
Coupling between FEC encoder/decoder and payload serialization—mitigated by treating FEC as a transparent layer above serialization
Coupling between network monitoring and adaptive parameter tuning—mitigated by defining monitoring as a separate concern that feeds into a policy engine
Coupling between critical vs. non-critical frame classification and the hybrid strategy—mitigated by externalizing frame priority rules to configuration
coupling_check.notes: DP6 achieves low coupling by separating concerns: (1) detection (network monitoring), (2) classification (frame priority rules), (3) mitigation (selective retransmission + FEC), and (4) adaptation (policy engine). Each can be evolved independently. Dual-path failover (DP3) is orthogonal and can be composed with DP6 if needed.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss target (≤1% or alternative) is measurable and stable during agreed network conditions; RISK: If network conditions are highly variable or unmeasurable, adaptive tuning may not converge.
ASSUMPTION: Critical control frames can be reliably identified and prioritized; RISK: Misclassification of frame priority could degrade safety or responsiveness.

ASSUMPTION: Retransmission latency budget exists (i.e., operator can tolerate bounded delay for non-critical telemetry); RISK: If latency is extremely tight, retransmission overhead may violate real-time constraints.
ASSUMPTION: FEC overhead (redundancy) is acceptable within bandwidth constraints; RISK: If bandwidth is severely limited, FEC may not be viable; selective retransmission alone may be required.
ASSUMPTION: Network monitoring infrastructure (packet counters, loss statistics) is available; RISK: If monitoring is unavailable or unreliable, adaptive tuning cannot function.
ASSUMPTION: Customer configuration interface is stable and well-defined; RISK: Frequent configuration changes or unclear semantics could introduce operational errors.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable retransmission latency budget (e.g., 'control frames must be retransmitted within X ms'), (2) bandwidth constraints (if any) that bound FEC overhead, (3) definition of 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., latency range, jitter profile, loss distribution), and (4) whether network monitoring is operator-visible or internal. Additionally, clarify whether the 1% loss target applies to raw link loss or end-to-end application-level loss after mitigation. This will enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping and reduce risk of over-engineering or under-specification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates network resilience logic including selective retransmission for critical control frames, configurable forward error correction for telemetry/status streams, network condition monitoring, and adaptive parameter adjustment based on customer-defined loss thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control frames; Teleoperation applies selective retransmission strategy to ensure critical commands reach Vehicle with high reliability.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits telemetry and status streams with configurable FEC applied; Teleoperation monitors outbound stream quality and adjusts FEC parameters based on network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety signals treated as critical control frames; Teleoperation ensures safety alerts use selective retransmission; receives feedback on delivery confirmation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation continuously monitors network conditions (loss rates, latency, jitter) via Network Link; adjusts retransmission and FEC parameters dynamically based on observed conditions and customer-defined thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural home for network resilience logic governing command delivery and telemetry robustness. Selective retransmission of operator commands and safety signals aligns with Teleoperation's responsibility for reliable command-and-control. FEC for telemetry streams is a direct extension of Teleoperation's data transmission role. Network monitoring and adaptive adjustment are core to maintaining teleoperation quality. Minor diffusion: customer-defined thresholds may require configuration interface to external settings management, but logic remains contained.

Partial fit. Safety Services owns safety signal generation and validation, but the hybrid loss mitigation strategy is broader than safety alone—it covers telemetry and general control frames. Placing the entire DP here would create coupling to non-safety telemetry streams and general network adaptation logic that belongs in Teleoperation. Safety Services should consume the retransmission guarantees provided by Teleoperation, not own the mechanism.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the remote platform receiving commands and generating telemetry. The hybrid loss mitigation strategy is a network-layer concern between operator and vehicle, not a vehicle-internal function. Placing this logic in Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network resilience policy, making it difficult to adjust strategies without vehicle updates. Vehicle should be agnostic to how commands arrive reliably.

Moderate fit but overly broad. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could technically host the logic, doing so bypasses the natural architectural layer (Teleoperation) that owns command-and-control reliability. Placing the DP at NGHE Solution level would diffuse ownership and make the architecture less analyzable. Better to keep it at the Teleoperation level and have NGHE Solution orchestrate configuration.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The hybrid loss mitigation strategy is fundamentally a teleoperation network resilience concern. Selective retransmission of operator commands and safety signals directly supports Teleoperation's mission to deliver reliable remote control. Configurable FEC for telemetry streams is a natural extension of Teleoperation's responsibility for bidirectional data integrity. Continuous network monitoring and adaptive adjustment are core to maintaining teleoperation quality under variable network conditions. Containment is strong because all four pillars of the DP (selective retransmission, FEC, monitoring, adaptation) operate within the Teleoperation domain boundary without requiring deep coupling to Vehicle internals or Safety Services logic.
containment_risks: Customer-defined loss thresholds and FEC parameters may require external configuration management; ensure Teleoperation exposes a clean configuration interface rather than embedding policy.

Safety Services must be able to mark frames as 'critical' for selective retransmission; define a clear contract (e.g., frame tagging or priority queue) between Safety Services and Teleoperation to avoid tight coupling.

Network Link interface must provide sufficient observability (loss rates, latency, jitter) for Teleoperation to make adaptive decisions; if Network Link does not expose these metrics, Teleoperation will diffuse into network diagnostics.
Vehicle must acknowledge receipt of critical commands reliably; ensure feedback path from Vehicle through Teleoperation is well-defined to avoid Teleoperation becoming a Vehicle-internal concern.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) is well-positioned to instrument and verify this criterion through continuous network monitoring and adaptive parameter adjustment. Test harnesses can inject packet loss at the Network Link interface and validate that selective retransmission and FEC maintain the target threshold. Traceability from FR → DP → IM is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Teleoperation module owns all four pillars of the loss mitigation strategy (selective retransmission, FEC, monitoring, adaptation) with a containment score of 0.92. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous: Teleoperation is responsible for maintaining communication integrity; Safety Services consumes the guarantees; Vehicle remains agnostic to network resilience mechanisms. The external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and support clear allocation of responsibilities.

diffusion_risks: Customer-defined loss thresholds and FEC parameters risk diffusion if embedded as hardcoded values in Teleoperation. Mitigation: expose a clean configuration interface (e.g., ConfigurationService or ParameterStore) that Teleoperation consumes at runtime, allowing threshold updates without code changes.

Safety Services frame tagging contract is implicit. Risk: if Safety Services and Teleoperation lack a formal interface for marking critical frames, selective retransmission logic may diffuse into Safety Services or become duplicated. Mitigation: define explicit frame priority/criticality tagging protocol (e.g., frame header field or priority queue enumeration) as a formal contract between modules.

Network Link observability is assumed but not guaranteed. Risk: if Network Link does not expose loss rates, latency, and jitter metrics, Teleoperation will diffuse into network diagnostics or require tight coupling to low-level network drivers. Mitigation: ensure Network Link interface explicitly publishes NetworkConditionMetrics (loss_rate, latency_ms, jitter_ms) as a required output.

Vehicle acknowledgment feedback path is mentioned but not detailed. Risk: if Vehicle does not reliably acknowledge receipt of critical commands, Teleoperation may diffuse into Vehicle-internal retry logic or timeout handling. Mitigation: define a formal AckFrame protocol with timeout and retry semantics at the Teleoperation boundary, not within Vehicle.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the natural and correct home for this DP. The module encapsulates all network resilience logic without requiring deep coupling to Vehicle internals or Safety Services implementation details. The 0.92 containment score reflects strong architectural fit. Minor diffusion risks are manageable through interface contracts (configuration, frame tagging, network metrics, acknowledgment protocol) rather than architectural restructuring. Recommended approach: keep the DP in Teleoperation and harden the four external interfaces to eliminate ambiguity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. FR requires maintaining teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits under agreed network conditions. DP6 specifies a hybrid loss mitigation strategy (selective retransmission + configurable FEC + continuous monitoring + adaptive adjustment) that is fit-for-purpose. The DP is concrete enough to guide implementation (e.g., retransmission logic for critical frames, FEC codec selection, network metric collection, threshold-based parameter tuning) and measurable against the acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR and DP.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the frame criticality contract: Define a Frame Priority or Criticality enumeration (e.g., CRITICAL, STANDARD) that Safety Services applies to safety signals and Tasking applies to operator commands. Teleoperation uses this tag to route frames to selective retransmission vs. standard transmission paths. Document this contract in the Teleoperation-SafetyAlert and Teleoperation-Tasking interface specifications.

Expose Network Link metrics explicitly: Ensure the Network Link interface publishes a NetworkConditionMetrics structure (loss_rate_percent, latency_ms, jitter_ms, timestamp) at regular intervals. Teleoperation subscribes to this stream and uses it to trigger adaptive parameter adjustment. Define thresholds and adjustment rules (e.g., 'if loss_rate > 0.5%, increase FEC redundancy by 10%') as part of the Teleoperation design specification.

Externalize customer-defined thresholds: Create a ConfigurationService or ParameterStore interface that Teleoperation queries for customer-defined loss thresholds and FEC parameters. Avoid embedding these values in code. Document the parameter schema (e.g., target_packet_loss_percent, fec_redundancy_level, retransmission_timeout_ms) and how Teleoperation reloads them (on-demand or periodic polling).

Define Vehicle acknowledgment protocol: Specify an AckFrame message format and timeout/retry semantics for critical commands. Document whether Teleoperation or Vehicle owns retry logic (recommended: Teleoperation owns retransmission; Vehicle owns acknowledgment generation). Include failure modes (e.g., 'if critical command not acknowledged within 500ms, retransmit up to 3 times; if still no ack, escalate to Safety Services').

Add traceability annotation to DP6: Include explicit mapping to acceptance criterion: 'DP6 maintains packet loss ≤ 1% (or customer-defined target) by combining selective retransmission (targets critical frames with 99%+ delivery confidence) and configurable FEC (targets telemetry streams with tunable redundancy). Continuous monitoring enables adaptive adjustment to maintain target under variable network conditions.' This strengthens FR-DP-IM traceability for verification planning.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM allocation is excellent (0.92 containment), and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions expose four critical interface contracts that are currently implicit and must be formalized before implementation. Given high safety criticality, these contracts warrant explicit sub-FR decomposition to ensure each interface obligation is captured, allocated, and verifiable as a first-class requirement. Proceeding directly to implementation without formalizing these contracts risks diffusion, coupling, and verification gaps. Decomposing to the next level ensures each interface contract becomes a traceable, owned, and testable requirement.
next_level_sub_frs: FR6.1: Provide frame criticality tagging mechanism to enable selective retransmission routing. (Owned by: Safety Services + Tasking as producers; Teleoperation as consumer. DP: Frame Priority enumeration (CRITICAL, STANDARD) applied by upstream modules; Teleoperation routes CRITICAL frames to selective retransmission, STANDARD to standard transmission.)

FR6.2: Expose network condition metrics (loss rate, latency, jitter) to enable adaptive parameter adjustment. (Owned by: Network Link as producer; Teleoperation as consumer. DP: NetworkConditionMetrics structure published at regular intervals; Teleoperation subscribes and triggers adaptive tuning when thresholds exceeded.)

FR6.3: Externalize customer-defined packet loss thresholds and FEC parameters to enable runtime configuration without code changes. (Owned by: ConfigurationService or ParameterStore as producer; Teleoperation as consumer. DP: Parameter schema (target_packet_loss_percent, fec_redundancy_level, retransmission_timeout_ms) exposed via configuration interface; Teleoperation queries on-demand or periodic polling.)

FR6.4: Define Vehicle acknowledgment protocol and timeout/retry semantics for critical commands to ensure reliable delivery confirmation. (Owned by: Vehicle as acknowledgment producer; Teleoperation as retry/escalation owner. DP: AckFrame message format with timeout (e.g., 500ms) and retry logic (up to 3 retransmissions); escalate to Safety Services if ack not received after final retry.)

revise.why: DP6 is logically correct but underspecified in its interface contracts. The four recommended_revisions should be incorporated into the DP specification as explicit design decisions: (1) frame criticality tagging protocol, (2) network metrics publication contract, (3) configuration externalization mechanism, (4) acknowledgment protocol with timeout/retry semantics. This transforms implicit assumptions into explicit design parameters that can be allocated to sub-FRs and verified independently. Revised DP6 should read: 'Implement hybrid loss mitigation strategy (selective retransmission + configurable FEC + continuous monitoring + adaptive adjustment) with four formalized interface contracts: (a) Frame Priority tagging from Safety Services and Tasking, (b) NetworkConditionMetrics from Network Link, (c) ParameterStore for customer-defined thresholds, (d) AckFrame protocol with Vehicle for critical command confirmation.'

assumptions: Safety Services and Tasking modules are willing and able to tag frames with criticality level (CRITICAL vs. STANDARD) at the point of message generation.
Network Link interface can reliably measure and publish loss_rate, latency_ms, and jitter_ms at regular intervals (e.g., every 100ms or 1s).
A ConfigurationService or ParameterStore exists or can be created to manage customer-defined thresholds and FEC parameters without requiring Teleoperation code changes.
Vehicle can generate and return AckFrame messages for critical commands with deterministic timing (e.g., within 100-500ms of receipt).
Customer-defined packet loss targets are stable during a teleoperation session and do not require real-time renegotiation.
FEC codec selection (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and redundancy levels are known or can be determined at design time based on customer profile.
open_risks: Frame criticality tagging may not be uniformly applied by Safety Services and Tasking if the contract is not formally enforced (e.g., via interface definition language or runtime validation). Risk: selective retransmission logic may receive untagged frames and default to standard transmission, reducing safety margin. Mitigation: define tagging as a mandatory field in the message contract; add runtime assertions in Teleoperation to detect and log untagged frames.

Network Link metrics may be delayed, lossy, or unavailable during severe network degradation. Risk: Teleoperation's adaptive adjustment logic may operate on stale or missing data, leading to suboptimal parameter tuning. Mitigation: define fallback behavior (e.g., conservative FEC increase if metrics unavailable for >5s) and document acceptable metric staleness (e.g., ≤1s).

ConfigurationService may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if Teleoperation queries it synchronously on every session start. Risk: configuration delays or service unavailability could block teleoperation startup. Mitigation: cache configuration locally with periodic refresh; define timeout and fallback defaults.

Vehicle acknowledgment protocol may conflict with existing Vehicle communication patterns if Vehicle was not designed with explicit ack semantics. Risk: retrofitting ack logic into Vehicle may require deep coupling or protocol changes. Mitigation: validate Vehicle's current communication model early; if ack is not natively supported, design Teleoperation to infer ack from Vehicle state changes (e.g., command execution confirmation) as a fallback.

Timeout and retry parameters (e.g., 500ms timeout, 3 retries) are assumed but not derived from safety analysis. Risk: if timeout is too short, legitimate network delays cause false retransmissions; if too long, safety response is delayed. Mitigation: conduct FMEA or fault tree analysis to derive timeout and retry limits from safety requirements (e.g., maximum acceptable latency for safety-critical commands).

FEC redundancy level may be customer-specific and difficult to tune without empirical network testing. Risk: generic FEC parameters may be over-conservative (wasting bandwidth) or under-conservative (failing to meet loss target). Mitigation: design Teleoperation to support A/B testing or gradual parameter adjustment; provide telemetry to customer for tuning feedback.
evidence_needed: Formal interface specification for Frame Priority tagging (e.g., protobuf, OpenAPI, or UML contract) showing CRITICAL and STANDARD enumeration and how Safety Services and Tasking apply it.
Network Link interface specification documenting NetworkConditionMetrics structure, publication frequency, accuracy, and latency guarantees.
ConfigurationService or ParameterStore schema and API specification (e.g., REST endpoint, gRPC service, or file-based config) showing parameter names, types, ranges, and reload semantics.

Vehicle acknowledgment protocol specification (AckFrame format, timeout values, retry count, escalation rules) and evidence that Vehicle can support it (e.g., existing ack mechanism or design feasibility study).
Safety analysis (FMEA, fault tree, or hazard analysis) linking timeout/retry parameters to safety requirements and demonstrating that chosen values are adequate.
Test plan for each sub-FR showing how frame tagging, network metrics, configuration externalization, and acknowledgment protocol will be verified independently and in integration.
Traceability matrix linking FR6 → FR6.1, FR6.2, FR6.3, FR6.4 → respective DPs and IMs, with verification methods and acceptance criteria for each sub-FR.
